## Installing and importing packages

We install the core packages used throughout the course using pip, and learn how to import them into a script at the start of each session.

*Video will be embedded here.*

```python
# Purpose: verify that all course packages are installed and importable
# Note: packages must first be installed
#   In the Terminal, run this once:
#   pip install pandas openpyxl matplotlib statsmodels seaborn numpy

import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf
import seaborn as sns
import numpy as np

# Check versions
print("pandas:", pd.__version__)
print("numpy:", np.__version__)
print("All packages imported successfully.")
```

## Exercises

### Exercise 1.6.1
Open the Terminal in Positron (not the console) and install the following packages in a
single command: `pandas`, `openpyxl`, `matplotlib`, `statsmodels`, `seaborn`, and `numpy`.
If any packages are already installed, pip will skip them automatically.

<details>
<summary>Solution</summary>

In the Terminal tab, run:
```bash
pip install pandas openpyxl matplotlib statsmodels seaborn numpy
```
You should see output ending with `Successfully installed ...` or confirmation that the
packages are already up to date. If `pip` is not recognised on Mac, use `pip3` instead.
</details>

---

### Exercise 1.6.2
Create a new section in your `exercises_topic1.py` script with a comment header for
Topic 1.6. Write import statements for all six packages using the conventional aliases
used in the course, and run them. If any import fails with a `ModuleNotFoundError`, it
means that package was not installed correctly — go back and reinstall it via the Terminal.

<details>
<summary>Solution</summary>

```python
# ── 1.6 Installing and importing packages ─────────────────────────────────────

import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf
import seaborn as sns
import numpy as np
import openpyxl
```
No output and no errors means all imports succeeded. A `ModuleNotFoundError` on any line
means that package needs to be installed first via `pip install <package>` in the Terminal.
</details>

---

### Exercise 1.6.3
After your import statements, add code that prints the version number of `pandas` and
`numpy`. Then look up how you would check the version of `matplotlib` — the pattern is
the same across all packages.

<details>
<summary>Solution</summary>

```python
print("pandas:", pd.__version__)
print("numpy:", np.__version__)
print("matplotlib:", plt.matplotlib.__version__)
```
```
pandas: 2.2.3
numpy: 2.2.5
matplotlib: 3.10.1
```
The exact version numbers on your machine may differ. The `.__version__` attribute follows
the same pattern for virtually all Python packages.
</details>

---

### Exercise 1.6.4
The two statements below will both cause errors. Run each one and read the error message.
Explain in your own words what each error means and how to fix it.

```python
import Pandas as pd
```
```python
print(pd.DataFrame())
```
_(Run the second line in a fresh console session where pandas has not yet been imported.)_

<details>
<summary>Solution</summary>

**First error:**
```
ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'Pandas'
```
Python package names are case-sensitive. The correct name is `pandas` (all lowercase), not
`Pandas`. Fix: `import pandas as pd`.

**Second error:**
```
NameError: name 'pd' is not defined
```
Even though pandas is installed, it has not been imported in this session. The fix is to
add `import pandas as pd` at the top of the script before using it. This is why import
statements always go at the very top of every script — they must run before any code that
depends on them.
</details>

---

### Exercise 1.6.5
Run the following command in the Terminal and read the error message carefully. What does
it tell you? Then list the most common reasons why a `pip install` command might fail.

```bash
pip install pandaas
```

<details>
<summary>Solution</summary>

Running the command produces an error along the lines of:
```
ERROR: Could not find a version that satisfies the requirement pandaas
ERROR: No matching distribution found for pandaas
```
pip searched the Python Package Index (PyPI) and found no package with that name.

The most common reasons a `pip install` command fails are:

- **Typo in the package name** — package names are case-sensitive and must be spelled
  exactly right. `Pandas`, `PANDAS`, and `pandaas` will all fail; the correct name is
  `pandas`.
- **Wrong tool** — running `pip install` in the Python console instead of the Terminal.
  The console runs Python code; `pip` is a Terminal command. In Positron, make sure you
  are in the Terminal tab, not the console.
- **No internet connection** — pip downloads packages from PyPI, so installation requires
  an active internet connection.
- **pip not recognised** — on some Mac setups, `pip` is not on the PATH and you need to
  use `pip3` instead. Alternatively, `python -m pip install pandas` always works because
  it calls pip through Python directly.

</details>
